walkthrough.title: Choose Your Map
walkthrough.text: Let's get Concordia set up. First, unfold the game board. It is double-sided with two different maps. The Imperium side is the larger map for three to five players. The Italia side is the smaller, tighter map for two to four players. Pick the side that matches your player count and lay it flat in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Goods
walkthrough.text: Now let's organize the goods. There are five types: brick, food, tool, wine, and cloth. Brick is the most common and cheapest, while cloth is the rarest and most valuable. Sort them into separate piles near the board where everyone can reach them. These will serve as the general supply throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Province Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Look at the provinces on the board. Each province has a name and a goods icon showing what it produces. Place one matching goods token on the province bonus space for each province. These bonuses are claimed by the player who triggers production there using the Prefect card. We will explain that during rules.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Cities
walkthrough.text: Each city on the board has a letter and a goods type it produces. Place the matching goods tokens on each city tile to show the cost of building there. The cost varies by city type: brick cities cost the least, while cloth cities cost the most. Some cities also require additional goods combinations to build in them.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Personality Card Display
walkthrough.text: Find the personality cards that are not part of the starting hands. Lay out seven personality cards in a row next to the board to form the market display. These are the cards players can buy during the game using the Senator or Consul actions. The cards on the left are cheaper to buy, and the ones on the right cost more.

walkthrough.title: Stack Remaining Personality Cards
walkthrough.text: Take the remaining personality cards and stack them face up near the display. When a card is bought from the display, the remaining cards slide left and a new card fills in from this stack on the right side. The game can end when the last card from this supply enters the display and is purchased.

walkthrough.title: Place the Concordia Card
walkthrough.text: Find the special Concordia card and place it face up near the personality card stack. This card is claimed by the player who triggers the game end, either by buying the last display card or by building their fifteenth house. The Concordia card is worth seven victory points at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Storehouses
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes the matching storehouse board. This board has spaces for your goods supply, your coin stash, and your colonists. Place it in front of you where everyone can see your resources. Your storehouse is always public information.

walkthrough.title: Give Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player receives their starting goods and coins. Everyone gets a small supply of goods to start: one of each of cloth, wine, tool, food, and brick. You also receive a starting amount of money. Place everything on your storehouse board. The exact amounts are printed on the back of the rulebook for reference.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Colonists
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with one land colonist and one sea colonist on the board. The land colonist is a person-shaped piece and the sea colonist is a ship. Place both of your colonists in the starting city, which is Roma on the Imperium map or the marked starting city on the Italia map. All players begin in the same city.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Houses
walkthrough.text: Each player also places one house in the starting city. Take one house piece from your supply and put it on one of the open building slots in that city. This is your only free house placement. All future houses must be built using the Architect card and paid for with goods.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Personality Cards
walkthrough.text: Give each player their identical starting hand of seven personality cards: Tribune, Architect, Prefect, Colonist, Mercator, Diplomat, and Senator. These are your action cards. On each turn you will play exactly one card from your hand to take that action. Look through them to familiarize yourself with the names.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Praefectus Magnus Tile
walkthrough.text: Place the Praefectus Magnus tile near the board. This tile is given to the player who first triggers production in any province using their Prefect card. It provides a double province bonus. It passes to any player who later uses the alternate Prefect action, so it will change hands throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Determine Player Order
walkthrough.text: Let's figure out who goes first. The rules suggest the youngest player starts, but you can pick randomly if you prefer. Going first is a small advantage, so later players receive slightly more starting resources to compensate. Seat order matters because play goes clockwise. Once everyone is settled, you are ready to learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: Concordia has a beautifully simple turn structure. On your turn, you do exactly one thing: play one personality card from your hand face up onto your personal discard pile, then carry out that card's action. That is it. No phases, no upkeep, no cleanup. Play a card, do its thing, next player. The catch is that once you play a card, it stays in your discard pile until you play Tribune to pick everything back up.

walkthrough.title: Your Hand Is Your Action Menu
walkthrough.text: Think of your hand of personality cards as your action menu. Each card gives you a different action, and you can only use each one once before you need to recover your hand. As you buy new cards from the display, your menu grows. This is one of the core tensions in Concordia. Do you play a card now because you need the action, or do you save it because you might need it more later?

walkthrough.title: The Tribune Card
walkthrough.text: Let's start with Tribune because it is the reset button. When you play Tribune, you pick up all the cards from your discard pile back into your hand. You also gain one coin for each card you pick up, not counting the Tribune itself. If you have fewer than three cards left when you play Tribune, you may also place one new colonist from your supply onto the board for free. Tribune is the only way to recover your played cards, so timing it well is crucial.

walkthrough.title: The Architect Card
walkthrough.text: The Architect lets you move your colonists and build houses. First, you may move each of your colonists up to a certain number of steps along the routes on the board. Land colonists travel along roads, and sea colonists travel along sea routes. Then, for each colonist that ends in a city where you do not already have a house, you may build one house there by paying the required goods. The goods cost is shown on the city tile.

walkthrough.title: Building Costs and Crowding
walkthrough.text: When you build a house in a city, you pay the goods cost printed on the city tile. But there is a twist: the coin cost depends on how crowded the city is. You must pay coins equal to the total number of houses in that city after you build, including your own. So if you are the first to build there, you pay one coin. If two houses are already there, you pay three coins. This crowding multiplier makes popular cities increasingly expensive.

walkthrough.title: The Prefect Card
walkthrough.text: The Prefect triggers production in a province. When you play Prefect, choose any province on the board. Every player who has at least one house in a city within that province receives the matching goods token for each of their houses there. Then you, the active player, also take the province bonus tile from that province. This bonus is one extra goods token matching the province type. After a province has been activated, flip its bonus tile to the coin side.

walkthrough.title: Prefect Coin Option
walkthrough.text: Instead of producing a province, you can choose to use the alternate Prefect action: collect coins from all flipped province tiles. Each province whose bonus tile is on the coin side gives you one coin. Then flip all those tiles back to their goods side, resetting them for future production. You also claim the Praefectus Magnus tile when you use this option. Timing this well can net you a big pile of coins in the mid-game.

walkthrough.title: The Praefectus Magnus
walkthrough.text: The Praefectus Magnus tile is a powerful bonus that changes hands during the game. Whoever holds it receives double the province bonus when they produce a province with the Prefect card. So instead of one bonus goods token, you get two. You lose the tile when another player uses the coin-collecting Prefect option. It adds an interesting push-and-pull dynamic to production timing.

walkthrough.title: The Colonist Card
walkthrough.text: The Colonist card lets you add new colonists to the board. When you play it, you may pay food and tools to place new colonists from your supply onto the board. A land colonist costs one food and one tool. A sea colonist costs one food and one tool as well. New colonists are placed in the starting city. Alternatively, you can skip placing colonists and simply take five coins plus one coin per colonist you have on the board.

walkthrough.title: The Mercator Card
walkthrough.text: The Mercator lets you trade goods at the market. When you play this card, you first gain three coins from the supply. Then you may make up to two trades, buying or selling goods at the fixed market prices. Brick costs three coins, food costs four, tools cost five, wine costs six, and cloth costs seven. You can buy goods you need or sell goods you do not. Each trade is one type of good, any quantity.

walkthrough.title: The Diplomat Card
walkthrough.text: The Diplomat lets you copy another player's last played card. Look at the top card of each opponent's discard pile. Choose one and perform that action as if you had played that card yourself. The original owner keeps their card. This is a powerful card that gives you flexibility because you can borrow any action that is currently visible on top of someone's discard. It does not work on Tribune.

walkthrough.title: The Senator Card
walkthrough.text: The Senator lets you buy personality cards from the display. You may purchase up to two cards from the seven-card market display. The first card costs its printed price plus one coin for each card to its left in the display. The second card costs its price plus an additional coin for the same positional rule. New cards go directly into your hand, ready to use immediately. After purchasing, slide remaining cards left and refill from the stack.

walkthrough.title: The Consul Card
walkthrough.text: The Consul works like a discount Senator. When you play Consul, you may buy exactly one personality card from the display. The difference is that you do not pay the positional cost — you only pay the card's base price. This makes the Consul ideal for snagging expensive cards that are far to the right in the display, where the Senator's positional surcharge would make them very costly.

walkthrough.title: Specialist Cards
walkthrough.text: As you buy cards from the display, you will encounter specialist versions of the Prefect card. These include the Mason, Farmer, Smith, Vintner, and Weaver. Each specialist produces goods only from cities of their matching type. When you play a Mason, all your houses in brick cities produce brick. When you play a Vintner, all your houses in wine cities produce wine. Specialists let you produce without activating an entire province, and they do not benefit opponents.

walkthrough.title: Colonist Movement Rules
walkthrough.text: Let's clarify how colonists move. Land colonists move along roads connecting cities on land. Sea colonists move along sea routes connecting coastal cities and islands. Each colonist can move up to a number of steps based on the Architect card you played. Colonists can pass through cities without stopping. Multiple colonists from any players can share the same city. You only build where a colonist ends its movement.

walkthrough.title: House Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: You can only build a house in a city if your colonist is there and you do not already have a house in that city. Each player can have at most one house per city. Cities have a limited number of building slots, but in practice most cities have room for all players. Remember the crowding cost: coins equal to total houses in the city after building. The fifteenth house you build from your supply can trigger the game end.

walkthrough.title: Game End Triggers
walkthrough.text: The game can end in one of two ways. First, when a player builds their fifteenth house from their personal supply, they immediately take the Concordia card and earn those seven bonus points. Second, when the last personality card from the supply stack enters the display and is bought, that buyer takes the Concordia card. Either way, after the trigger, every other player gets exactly one more turn. Then the game ends and scoring begins.

walkthrough.title: God-Based Scoring
walkthrough.text: Scoring in Concordia is unique and surprising for new players. You do not score during the game at all. At the end, each personality card in your hand and discard pile has a god symbol. Each god scores based on different achievements. Vesta scores for goods and coins. Jupiter scores for houses in non-brick cities. Saturn scores for provinces where you have houses. Mercury scores for goods types you can produce. Mars scores for colonists on the board. Minerva scores based on the specialist type. The more cards with a matching god you have, the higher that god's multiplier.

walkthrough.title: How God Scoring Works
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through an example. Say you have three cards with the Jupiter symbol. Jupiter scores for each house you have built in a non-brick city. If you have four such houses, each Jupiter card scores four points, giving you twelve points from Jupiter alone. The key insight is that buying cards is not just about gaining actions — every card you buy also multiplies your end-game score. This is why card selection and building strategy must align.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Summary
walkthrough.text: To calculate your final score, group all your personality cards by their god symbol. Score each god separately by multiplying the number of matching cards by your achievements for that god. Add the Concordia card bonus if you have it. The player with the highest total wins. In case of a tie, the player with the most goods and coins combined wins. Concordia is a game where your final score often surprises you, so always do the math carefully.

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Concordia! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with four example players. Player 1 through Player 4 will each demonstrate different strategies and actions. One player will make some common mistakes so you can learn what to avoid. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Everyone starts in the same city with one house, one land colonist, and one sea colonist. Each player has the same seven personality cards in hand and the same starting goods. The board is wide open, and the race to expand begins now. Let's see how each player approaches their first few turns.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Architect
walkthrough.text: Player 1 starts strong by playing the Architect card. They move their land colonist two cities along the road to a nearby food city. Then they pay the required goods and one coin to build a house there. Player 1 is the first to build in that city, so the crowding cost is just one coin. Smart move — they now have two houses and can produce in two locations.

walkthrough.title: Why Early Expansion Matters
walkthrough.text: Building houses early is critical in Concordia. Houses generate goods when provinces produce, and more houses means more resources flowing in. The earlier you build, the more production cycles your houses participate in. Player 1 is already set up to benefit every time that food province is activated by any player's Prefect card.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Prefect
walkthrough.text: Player 2 plays the Prefect card and chooses the province containing the starting city. Every player has a house there, so everyone produces one good. Player 2 also takes the province bonus tile for an extra good. Notice how the Prefect benefits everyone with houses in that province — but Player 2 gets the bonus on top.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: Colonist
walkthrough.text: Player 3 plays the Colonist card and pays one food and one tool to place a new sea colonist in the starting city. Having a second sea colonist lets Player 3 reach distant coastal cities faster. Multiple colonists moving simultaneously is a powerful way to expand across the map quickly.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Turn: The First Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 4 looks at the personality card display and spots a powerful specialist card on the far right. They play the Senator card and buy it. The problem? The card was in position seven, making the positional surcharge enormous. Player 4 spent almost all their coins on a single card when they have barely any houses on the board to take advantage of it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Buy Cards That Match Your Board
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is that personality cards are scoring multipliers, not just action upgrades. Buying cards purely for their actions without considering how they score at game end is a common trap. Player 4 bought a specialist card but has no houses of that type on the board yet. That card will not score well unless Player 4 invests heavily in building the matching house type.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Mercator
walkthrough.text: Player 1 plays the Mercator card. They gain three coins from the supply, then sell some brick they do not need and buy wine for their next building project. The Mercator is your economic engine — it converts goods you have into goods you need. Player 1 is setting up to build in a wine city next turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Architect
walkthrough.text: Player 2 plays Architect and moves their sea colonist along the coast to a tool city. They build a house there, paying the goods cost plus one coin for being the first builder. Player 2 now has houses producing two different goods types. Diversifying early gives you flexibility in what you can build and trade later.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: Senator
walkthrough.text: Player 3 plays the Senator card and buys two cards from the display. They carefully select cards from the left side of the display where the positional costs are low. One card has a Jupiter god symbol and the other has a Saturn symbol. Player 3 is thinking ahead about scoring — they plan to build houses that align with both gods.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Second Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 4 wants to build a house but notices a wine city with two houses already in it. The goods are not bad, and the city is close. They play Architect, move there, and build. But the crowding cost is now three coins because there will be three total houses in that city. Player 4 just spent precious coins that could have been used on a nearby empty city for just one coin.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Avoid Crowded Cities Early
walkthrough.text: Building in crowded cities early in the game is usually a mistake. The coin multiplier makes it expensive, and there are plenty of empty cities available. Save crowded-city builds for later when you have more coins and when the strategic location truly justifies the cost. An empty city costs only one coin — that savings adds up fast over multiple builds.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Prefect
walkthrough.text: Player 1 plays the Prefect card on the province with the food city where they just built a house. Their house produces food, and they claim the province bonus for an extra food. Meanwhile, any other player with houses in that province also produces. Player 1 is steadily building their resource engine.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Diplomat
walkthrough.text: Player 2 plays the Diplomat card and copies Player 1's Prefect action. They choose a different province to produce. This is a clever move — Player 2 gets the Prefect benefit without spending their own Prefect card, saving it for later. The Diplomat is one of the most flexible cards in the game because it lets you use any action that is visible on top of an opponent's discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: Tribune Recovery
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has played four cards and only has three left. They decide to play Tribune now to recover everything. They pick up their four discarded cards and gain four coins. That is a nice cash infusion. Player 3 now has a full hand of nine cards — the original seven plus the two they bought.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Third Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 4 still has five cards in hand but only one or two useful ones for the current situation. Instead of playing Tribune to recover, they keep spending less-useful cards on marginal actions — a Mercator trade they did not really need, a Colonist for coins instead of expansion. Two turns later, Player 4 finally plays Tribune with only one card to recover, gaining just one coin. They wasted turns on low-value plays by delaying Tribune too long.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Time Your Tribune Well
walkthrough.text: Do not delay Tribune until you are completely out of useful plays. Playing Tribune when you have three or four discarded cards is usually the sweet spot — you get a decent coin bonus and a full hand of options. Waiting too long means you are spending turns on weak actions just to avoid the Tribune. Worse, you miss out on playing your best cards multiple times throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check-In
walkthrough.text: Let's look at where everyone stands. Player 1 has three houses, steady production, and a balanced resource flow. Player 2 has built in diverse city types and used Diplomat cleverly. Player 3 has extra cards in hand and is setting up for a big scoring push. Player 4 is behind due to overspending on a card, building in a crowded city, and poor Tribune timing — but the game is far from over.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Recovery Plan
walkthrough.text: Player 4 needs to recover. The best approach is to focus on building houses in empty cities that match the specialist card they bought earlier. If they concentrate on one goods type and build three or four houses there, that specialist card starts pulling its weight both for production and for Minerva scoring. Sometimes a bad early buy can become a good strategy if you commit to it.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: The game continues with players taking turns playing one card at a time. The rhythm of Concordia is play cards, build houses, produce goods, buy new cards, and recover with Tribune. Keep an eye on two things: how many houses you have left to place, and how many cards remain in the supply stack. Either one running out can trigger the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Approaching Game End
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, start counting the cards in the supply stack and the houses remaining in each player's supply. When someone is close to placing their fifteenth house or the card supply is nearly empty, the end is near. This is when you should shift from engine building to maximizing your scoring multipliers. Make sure every card you buy aligns with your strongest god category.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Surprise
walkthrough.text: When the game ends and you tally god scores, Concordia often delivers surprises. Player 3, who quietly bought well-aligned cards, might surge ahead of Player 1 who had more houses. Player 4's specialist card finally pays off if they committed to matching houses. The lesson is that the cards you buy matter just as much as the houses you build. Both are essential for victory.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations — you have walked through the key turns of a Concordia game! You have seen all seven starting card actions, watched Player 4 learn from three common mistakes, and understood the rhythm of play, produce, buy, recover. Remember: do not buy cards that do not match your board, avoid crowded cities early, and time your Tribune recovery well.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all card types and scoring gods.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Wasted coins on unnecessary crowding premium

summary.title: Lesson: Avoid Crowded Cities Early
bullets: Crowding multiplier makes popular cities expensive
Empty cities cost only 1 coin — savings add up fast
Save crowded-city builds for late game when location matters more

bullets: Copy Player 1's Prefect → produce a different province
Gets Prefect benefit without spending own Prefect card

bullets: Played 4 cards → plays Tribune to recover all
Picks up 4 cards + gains 4 coins
Now has full hand of 9 cards (7 starting + 2 bought)

bullets: Has 5 cards left but only 1-2 useful ones
Keeps playing marginal actions instead of recovering via Tribune
Finally plays Tribune with only 1 card to recover → 1 coin

Delaying = wasting turns on weak plays for no benefit

Watch remaining houses in your supply

Near end: shift from engine building to scoring optimization

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for cards and gods
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we dive into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Concordia players make. First, buying personality cards purely for their actions without considering which god they score for — every card is a scoring multiplier at game end. Second, building in crowded cities early when empty cities are available for much less coin. Third, delaying Tribune until you have run out of useful cards and waste turns on low-value plays. Fourth, ignoring the endgame triggers and getting caught with unspent resources. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Expand Early and Often
walkthrough.text: Houses are the foundation of everything in Concordia. They generate goods when provinces produce, they score points through Jupiter and Saturn, and each one you build brings you closer to triggering the game end with the Concordia card. In the early game, prioritize getting houses on the board over buying cards or hoarding resources. A house that has been on the board for the whole game generates far more value than one built in the final few turns. Move your colonists aggressively and build whenever you can afford to.

walkthrough.title: Align Your Cards and Houses
walkthrough.text: The most important principle in Concordia is alignment between the cards you buy and the houses you build. If you are buying Jupiter cards, build houses in non-brick cities. If you are collecting Saturn cards, spread your houses across as many provinces as possible. If you pick up specialist cards, make sure you have houses of that matching goods type. Every card purchase should make you think: does my board position support this card's god scoring? If the answer is no, that card might not be worth buying.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Coins
walkthrough.text: Coins are the hidden bottleneck in Concordia. You need them for building houses, buying cards from the display, and they even factor into your Vesta scoring at game end. New players often burn through coins early on expensive builds and card purchases, then find themselves unable to act. The Mercator card is your friend here — it gives you three coins every time you play it, plus the ability to trade. Tribune also generates coins when you recover cards. Keep a healthy coin reserve and you will always have options.

walkthrough.title: Use the Diplomat Wisely
walkthrough.text: The Diplomat is one of the most underrated cards in the starting hand. It lets you copy any action sitting on top of an opponent's discard pile. This means you effectively get two uses of your strongest cards — your own play plus a Diplomat copy. Pay attention to what your opponents have recently played. If someone just used Prefect on a province where you have houses, great — but you can use Diplomat to Prefect a different province yourself. The Diplomat also lets you delay playing your own key cards.

walkthrough.title: Time Your Tribune Carefully
walkthrough.text: Tribune timing separates good players from great ones. Play Tribune too early and you miss out on actions you could have taken. Play it too late and you waste turns on marginal plays while your best cards sit in the discard pile. The sweet spot is usually when you have three to five cards discarded and none of your remaining cards are useful for the current situation. Remember that Tribune also gives you coins — one per recovered card — so recovering a large discard pile can be a significant cash infusion.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Game End Triggers
walkthrough.text: Concordia can end suddenly if you are not paying attention. Count the cards remaining in the supply stack and the houses in each player's personal supply. When someone has only two or three houses left, start preparing for the endgame. Shift your focus from building your engine to maximizing your scoring multipliers. Buy cards that align with your strongest god category. Make sure your goods and coins are not sitting idle — they score through Vesta, so spend or hold strategically.

walkthrough.title: Production Is a Shared Benefit
walkthrough.text: Remember that when anyone plays Prefect, all players with houses in that province produce goods. This means your opponents fund your engine every time they produce a province where you have houses. Build houses in provinces that other players are likely to activate. If two opponents have houses in a province, they will both want to produce there, and you will benefit for free. Conversely, be mindful that producing a province helps your opponents too — sometimes it is worth using the coin-collection Prefect instead to deny them goods.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't buy cards without considering their god-scoring alignment
Don't build in crowded cities when empty ones are available
Don't delay Tribune until you're out of useful cards
Don't ignore endgame triggers — watch houses and card supply

Move colonists aggressively — build whenever you can afford to
Building 15 houses triggers game end + Concordia card (7 VP)

Saturn → spread houses across many provinces
Minerva → build houses matching your specialist card types
Every card purchase should match your board position

summary.title: Manage Your Coins

Mercator gives 3 coins + trading ability every play
Tribune generates 1 coin per recovered card
Keep a healthy reserve — being broke = being stuck

summary.title: Use the Diplomat Wisely
bullets: Copy any action visible on top of an opponent's discard pile
Effectively doubles your strongest card's usage

Lets you delay playing your own key cards

Too early = missed actions, too late = wasted turns
Coins from recovery (1 per card) can be significant cash infusion
Frequent Tribune cycling lets you replay best cards more often

When someone has 2-3 houses left, shift to scoring mode
Buy cards aligning with your strongest god category

summary.title: Production Is a Shared Benefit
bullets: Any player's Prefect triggers production for ALL houses in that province
Build where opponents are likely to produce — free goods for you
Coin-collection Prefect can deny opponents production
Popular provinces get produced often — great for freeloading

walkthrough.title: The Cloth Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: Cloth is the most valuable goods type in Concordia. It sells for seven coins at the market and cloth cities are expensive to build in, but the payoff is enormous. A cloth rush strategy focuses on racing to cloth cities early, building houses there, and using Weaver specialist cards to produce cloth repeatedly. Even two or three cloth houses generating consistently can fund your entire game. Pair this with Mercator trades to convert excess cloth into whatever you need.

walkthrough.title: The Province Spread Strategy
walkthrough.text: Saturn scores points for the number of different provinces where you have at least one house. The province spread strategy prioritizes getting one house in as many provinces as possible, even if those houses are in cheap brick cities. This requires multiple colonists and aggressive Architect plays. With three or four Saturn cards and houses in eight or nine provinces, Saturn alone can score twenty-five to thirty-five points. Combine with Mercury, which rewards having houses producing many different goods types.

walkthrough.title: The Jupiter Power Play
walkthrough.text: Jupiter scores for houses in non-brick cities, meaning food, tool, wine, and cloth cities all count. The Jupiter strategy aims to build as many houses as possible while avoiding brick cities entirely. This means your houses are naturally more expensive to build, but each one contributes to a high Jupiter multiplier. Three or four Jupiter cards with ten or more qualifying houses can deliver forty or more points from Jupiter alone. The key is having enough resources to sustain expensive builds.

walkthrough.title: Specialist Engine Building
walkthrough.text: Specialist cards are the backbone of many winning strategies. The Minerva god scores based on matching specialist types, so stacking multiple copies of one specialist creates both a production engine and a scoring engine. For example, if you have three Weaver cards and four cloth houses, each Weaver produces four cloth, and Minerva scores twelve points. Choose a specialist type early, then focus your building on matching cities. Specialists produce without benefiting opponents, unlike the Prefect.

walkthrough.title: Diplomat Chains
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use the Diplomat not just as a spare action but as a strategic tool. When you see an opponent play a powerful card, wait to copy it with your Diplomat next turn. This effectively gives you access to actions you have already used without burning Tribune. Pay attention to the rhythm of the table — if multiple players have Prefects on top of their discard piles, you can produce different provinces each cycle using only your Diplomat. This saves your own Prefect for a critical province you want to time precisely.

walkthrough.title: Tribune Tempo Control
walkthrough.text: Mastering Tribune timing is an advanced skill. Fast Tribune cycling — playing Tribune after just three or four card plays — lets you replay your best cards more often. If your strongest play is an Architect build, fast cycling means more builds per game. Some players intentionally keep their hand small and cycle quickly, using the coin bonus from Tribune as supplementary income. The counterpoint is that bigger hands with more bought cards give more options per cycle. Find the balance that matches your board position.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing and the Concordia Card
walkthrough.text: Controlling when the game ends is a powerful strategic lever. If you are ahead on scoring multipliers, triggering the game end before opponents can catch up is valuable. The Concordia card awards seven bonus points to the player who triggers the end, making it worth racing for. Building your fifteenth house is the most common trigger, so count your remaining houses carefully. If you are behind, slow the game by avoiding builds that accelerate the end.

walkthrough.title: Vesta Scoring Optimization
walkthrough.text: Vesta is often overlooked by new players but can be a significant scoring category. Vesta scores based on your goods and coins remaining at game end. Each goods token is worth its market value divided by a factor, and coins count at face value. The key insight is that goods you do not spend are not wasted — they score through Vesta. In the final turns, if you cannot make a productive build or purchase, holding your resources for Vesta scoring can be better than forcing a suboptimal play. Combined with two or three Vesta cards, this can net fifteen to twenty points.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Card Display
walkthrough.text: The personality card display is a dynamic marketplace that reveals strategic information. Watch what cards opponents buy to infer their scoring strategy — if someone buys multiple Jupiter cards, they are targeting non-brick houses. Use this knowledge to compete for or avoid their provinces. Also note which gods are underrepresented in the display — if no Saturn cards are available, the province spread strategy is harder to build. Time your Senator and Consul plays for when the display offers cards that perfectly match your plan.

walkthrough.title: Mars and Colonist Optimization
walkthrough.text: Mars scores for the number of colonists you have on the board. Most players end the game with two or three colonists, so Mars often scores modestly. But if you invest in placing four or five colonists early and pair that with two or three Mars cards, you can score fifteen to twenty points from Mars while also gaining the movement advantage of multiple colonists for faster expansion. The Colonist card is underused by most players. Placing a third or fourth colonist early can open up parts of the map your opponents cannot reach efficiently.

entries.definition: Personality card. Recover all discarded cards back into your hand. Gain 1 coin per card recovered (excluding Tribune itself). If fewer than 3 cards in hand when played, you may also place 1 free colonist from your supply.

Personality card. Move your colonists along routes on the board, then build houses in cities where your colonists end their movement. Pay goods cost plus coin crowding multiplier for each house built.

Personality card. Choose a province to activate production — all players' houses in that province produce matching goods. Active player also takes the province bonus tile. Alternatively, collect coins from all flipped province tiles and claim the Praefectus Magnus.
Personality card. Pay 1 food + 1 tool per new colonist to place them in the starting city. Alternative action: take 5 coins plus 1 coin per colonist you have on the board.
Personality card. Gain 3 coins from the supply, then make up to 2 trades — buying or selling goods at fixed market prices. Each trade is one goods type in any quantity.
Personality card. Copy the top card of any opponent's discard pile and perform that action as your own. The original owner keeps their card. Cannot copy Tribune.
Personality card. Buy up to 2 cards from the 7-card market display. Cost = card's base price + 1 coin per card to its left. New cards go directly to your hand.
Personality card. Buy exactly 1 card from the market display at its base price only — no positional surcharge. Ideal for expensive cards on the right side of the display.
Specialist personality card. When played, all your houses in brick cities produce 1 brick each. Does not benefit opponents. Scores via Minerva god based on number of brick houses you have.
Specialist personality card. When played, all your houses in food cities produce 1 food each. Does not benefit opponents. Scores via Minerva god based on number of food houses you have.
Specialist personality card. When played, all your houses in tool cities produce 1 tool each. Does not benefit opponents. Scores via Minerva god based on number of tool houses you have.
Specialist personality card. When played, all your houses in wine cities produce 1 wine each. Does not benefit opponents. Scores via Minerva god based on number of wine houses you have.
Specialist personality card. When played, all your houses in cloth cities produce 1 cloth each. Does not benefit opponents. Scores via Minerva god based on number of cloth houses you have.

Scoring god. Scores based on your remaining goods and coins at game end. Each goods token is worth its market value, and coins count at face value, all divided by a scoring factor. Rewards holding resources rather than spending them at the very end.
Scoring god. Each Jupiter card scores points equal to the number of houses you have in non-brick cities (food, tool, wine, and cloth cities). Rewards building in expensive, diverse city types.
Scoring god. Each Saturn card scores points equal to the number of different provinces where you have at least one house. Rewards geographic spread across the map.

Scoring god. Each Mercury card scores points equal to the number of different goods types your houses can produce. Having houses in all five goods types maximizes Mercury scoring. Rewards production diversity.
Scoring god. Each Mars card scores points equal to the number of colonists you have on the board. Rewards investing in additional colonists beyond the starting two.

Scoring god. Each specialist card (Mason, Farmer, Smith, Vintner, Weaver) scores Minerva points equal to the number of houses you have of that specialist's matching goods type. Rewards specializing in one goods type.

Concordia's unique scoring system. Each personality card has a god symbol. At game end, group cards by god and multiply the count by your matching achievement. No points are scored during the game — everything is tallied at the end.
Special card worth 7 victory points, awarded to the player who triggers the game end. Either by building their 15th house or by buying the last personality card from the supply.
The most common and cheapest goods type. Costs 3 coins at market. Brick cities are the cheapest to build in. Houses in brick cities do NOT count for Jupiter scoring.
Goods type costing 4 coins at market. Required (along with tools) to place new colonists. Houses in food cities count for Jupiter scoring.
Goods type costing 5 coins at market. Required (along with food) to place new colonists. Houses in tool cities count for Jupiter scoring.
Goods type costing 6 coins at market. More valuable and rarer than brick, food, or tool. Houses in wine cities count for Jupiter scoring.
The rarest and most valuable goods type. Costs 7 coins at market. Cloth cities are the most expensive to build in. Houses in cloth cities count for Jupiter scoring.
A person-shaped piece that moves along road routes connecting inland and coastal cities. Used with the Architect card to reach cities for building houses. Each player starts with one.
A ship-shaped piece that moves along sea routes connecting coastal cities and islands. Used with the Architect card to reach coastal cities. Each player starts with one.
New colonists are placed via the Colonist card by paying 1 food + 1 tool per colonist. All new colonists enter at the starting city. Having more colonists lets you expand faster across the map.

A region on the board containing one or more cities. When a Prefect activates a province, all players' houses in cities within that province produce goods. Each province has a bonus tile showing its goods type.

A tile on each province that grants 1 extra matching goods token to the player who activates production there via Prefect. After activation, the tile flips to its coin side. Flipped tiles are collected as coins when a player uses the alternative Prefect action.

When a Prefect card activates a province, every house in that province produces 1 matching goods token for its owner. All players benefit — not just the Prefect player. This is the primary way goods enter the game.

A tile that grants double province bonus (2 goods instead of 1) when the holder activates production via Prefect. Changes hands when another player uses the coin-collecting Prefect option. Adds strategic tension to production timing.

A row of 7 face-up personality cards available for purchase. Cards on the left cost less (base price + fewer positional coins). When a card is bought, remaining cards slide left and a new card fills from the supply stack on the right.

Face-up stack of personality cards next to the market display. Cards slide into the display as cards are purchased. When the last card from this stack is bought from the display, the game end is triggered.

When buying cards via Senator, you pay the card's base price plus 1 coin per card to its left in the display. Position 1 costs base price only, position 7 costs base + 6 coins. The Consul bypasses this surcharge entirely.

Wooden building pieces placed in cities on the board. Each player has 15 houses. Houses generate goods via production, score points through Jupiter and Saturn, and building the 15th house can trigger the game end.

When building a house, you must pay coins equal to the total number of houses in that city after your build. First builder pays 1 coin, second pays 2, third pays 3, and so on. Makes popular cities increasingly expensive.

Each city requires specific goods to build a house there, shown on the city tile. Brick cities require brick, food cities require food, etc. Higher-value goods types generally require more expensive combinations.
Each player's personal board tracking their goods supply, coin stash, and available colonists. All resources on the storehouse are public information visible to all players.
When a player builds their 15th and final house, they claim the Concordia card (7 VP). All other players then get exactly 1 more turn before final scoring begins.

When the last personality card from the supply stack enters the display and is purchased, the buyer claims the Concordia card (7 VP). All other players then get exactly 1 more turn before final scoring begins.
After an end-game trigger, each other player gets exactly 1 more turn. This final turn follows normal rules — play 1 personality card and execute its action. Use it to maximize scoring position.

Each player has a personal discard pile where played personality cards go face up. The top card is visible to all players and can be copied via the Diplomat. Cards stay here until recovered by Tribune.
The city where all players begin the game (Roma on the Imperium map). All starting colonists and houses are placed here. New colonists placed via the Colonist card also enter at this city.
The larger side of the double-sided game board, designed for 3 to 5 players. Features more provinces and cities than the Italia map, giving players more room to spread out.
The smaller side of the double-sided game board, designed for 2 to 4 players. A tighter map with fewer provinces, leading to more direct competition for city slots and production.
